title: Gingerbread men
subtitle: Soft-cut gingerbread for decorating
excerpt: This gingerbread is soft enough to eat without decoration but firm enough to hold a clean edge when cut and bake without spreading. The spiced dough is rolled thick, cut, and baked briefly. Decoration is optional but the flat surface takes icing well.
sourceNotes: Gingerbread has been baked in Britain since the medieval period, brought by spice-route traders and documented in English domestic cookery from the sixteenth century onward. The cut gingerbread figure (shaped biscuits of spiced ginger dough) became a fixture of British domestic baking from the Victorian period. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — gingerbread biscuits appear in the cakes chapter.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze undecorated, layered in a box. Defrost at room temperature and decorate before serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe easily — the dough keeps for 3 days in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 2 weeks in an airtight tin. Decorated biscuits keep for 5 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard-setting white icing made from icing sugar and egg white (or meringue powder). Used to decorate gingerbread because it dries to a firm, crack-resistant surface that holds detail well. For piping outlines and fine detail, use a stiffer consistency with less liquid; for flooding the surface, thin it with a few drops of water until it flows level within 10 seconds.

content.text: These gingerbread figures bake with a slight bite and soft centre. They can be eaten plain, but the flat surface is ideal for
decoration. Chill the dough before rolling — warm gingerbread sticks to everything and the cut shapes lose definition.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}}, {{soft-brown-sugar}}, and {{golden-syrup}} together in a small saucepan until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Combine {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} in a large bowl. Pour in the melted mixture and add the {{eggs}}. Mix to a smooth dough. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to about 5 mm thick. Cut out figures or shapes and place on lined baking trays, leaving 2 cm between them. Bake for 10–12 minutes until set and slightly darker at the edges — they will firm as they cool, so pull them while the centres still look slightly underdone. Transfer to a wire rack. Decorate once completely cool.
